_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: She has died under six different names and walked back into the room every time.

museum_objects.title: Peso Moneda Corriente Note
museum_objects.description: An early paper peso note issued by the Banco de las Provincias Unidas del Río de la Plata.
museum_objects.significance: This flimsy paper note is the first of six times Valentina would be reborn under a new name after her value collapsed — a pattern that would repeat for two centuries.
museum_objects.plain_language: One of the very first paper peso notes ever printed in Argentina, from the 1820s.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so. | IMAGE QA FAIL: The note is issued by 'La Provincia de Buenos Ayres' not 'Banco de las Provincias Unidas del Río de la Plata'

museum_objects.title: Picturesque Illustrations of Buenos Ayres and Montevideo
museum_objects.description: An illustrated travel account by British artist Emeric Essex Vidal depicting daily life and commerce in Buenos Aires.
museum_objects.significance: These illustrations show the world Valentina was born into — a young, chaotic port city about to gamble everything on its own currency.
museum_objects.plain_language: An illustrated book from 1820 showing what Buenos Aires looked like right before Argentina created its own currency.

resolved.title: Picturesque illustrations of Buenos Ayres and Monte Video, consisting of twenty-four views: accompanied with descriptions of the scenery, and of the costumes, manners, &c., of the inhabitants of those cities and their environs

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

display_alternatives.title: General view of Buenos Ayres from the Plaza de Toros - Emeric Essex Vidal - Picturesque illustrations of Buenos Ayres and Monte Video (1820).jpg

museum_objects.title: Eva Perón 100 Peso Note
museum_objects.description: An Argentine peso banknote featuring the image of Eva Perón, one of the country's most iconic historical figures.
museum_objects.significance: Valentina wearing the face of a woman as fiercely loved and fought over as she herself has always been — devalued again and again, but never forgotten.
museum_objects.plain_language: An Argentine banknote featuring Eva Perón, one of the most famous women in the country's history.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Casa Rosada (Presidential Palace)
museum_objects.description: The presidential palace of Argentina, site of countless economic policy announcements including currency crises and devaluations.
museum_objects.significance: Every one of Valentina's six deaths and rebirths was announced from balconies in or near this building.
museum_objects.plain_language: Argentina's presidential palace, where many of the country's dramatic currency crises were announced.

museum_objects.title: Cacerolazo Protest Photographs
museum_objects.description: Photographs documenting the mass pot-banging protests during Argentina's 2001 economic collapse.
museum_objects.significance: This is what it looks like when an entire country loses faith in Valentina all at once — pots and pans banged in the streets instead of trust in a currency.
museum_objects.plain_language: Photos of Argentines banging pots and pans in the streets during the country's massive 2001 economic collapse.
museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. world_map.map_description: Valentina's peso was born in a Buenos Aires bank in 1826 and has been reborn under new names again and again, from Baring Brothers' London loans to the 2001 collapse.

nodes.label: Buenos Aires (Banco de las Provincias Unidas)
nodes.description: Where the first Argentine peso was issued on January 28, 1826.

nodes.label: Casa Rosada
nodes.description: The presidential palace at the center of Argentina's political and economic upheavals.

nodes.label: London (Baring Brothers)
nodes.description: The British bank whose loans to Argentina in the 1820s-1890s created cycles of debt and devaluation.

nodes.label: The Pampas
nodes.description: Argentina's vast agricultural heartland, the economic engine behind the peso's value for over a century.

nodes.label: Washington, D.C. (IMF)
nodes.description: Site of numerous IMF bailout negotiations tied to Argentina's repeated currency crises.
